During the following off-season, the Spurs signed veterans [Chris Paul](/wiki/Chris_Paul "Chris Paul") and [Harrison Barnes](/wiki/Harrison_Barnes "Harrison Barnes").[[142]](#cite_note-142)[[143]](#cite_note-143) They also drafted [Stephon Castle](/wiki/Stephon_Castle "Stephon Castle") and Rob Dillingham, although Dillingham was later traded away to the Minnesota Timberwolves.[[144]](#cite_note-144) Near the start of the season, [Gregg Popovich](/wiki/Gregg_Popovich "Gregg Popovich") was announced indefinitely out for the season due to health issues and was replaced by [Mitch Johnson](/wiki/Mitch_Johnson_(basketball) "Mitch Johnson (basketball)").[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146) A few days before the trade deadline, the Spurs acquired All-Star point guard [De'Aaron Fox](/wiki/De%27Aaron_Fox "De'Aaron Fox") as part of a three-team deal with the [Chicago Bulls](/wiki/Chicago_Bulls "Chicago Bulls") and the [Sacramento Kings](/wiki/Sacramento_Kings "Sacramento Kings").[[147]](#cite_note-147) After 46 games, Wembanyama was announced to miss the rest of the season due to blood clots.[[148]](#cite_note-148) Following the season, Castle was named Rookie of the Year.[[149]](#cite_note-149) On May 2, 2025, Popovich announced that he would step down as the head coach but remain president of basketball operations.[[150]](#cite_note-150) Mitch Johnson was named the team's permanent head coach.[[151]](#cite_note-151)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio_Spurs&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Main articles: [Lakers–Spurs rivalry](/wiki/Lakers%E2%80%93Spurs_rivalry "Lakers–Spurs rivalry"), [Mavericks–Spurs rivalry](/wiki/Mavericks%E2%80%93Spurs_rivalry "Mavericks–Spurs rivalry"), [Spurs–Suns rivalry](/wiki/Spurs%E2%80%93Suns_rivalry "Spurs–Suns rivalry"), and [Rockets–Spurs rivalry](/wiki/Rockets%E2%80%93Spurs_rivalry "Rockets–Spurs rivalry")

### Los Angeles Lakers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio_Spurs&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Los Angeles Lakers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Spurs_vs_Lakers.jpg/250px-Spurs_vs_Lakers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spurs_vs_Lakers.jpg)

From 1999 to 2005, the Spurs and Lakers combined to win seven straight Western Conference titles.

From 1999 to 2004, the rivalry between the Spurs and the [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers") was considered the NBA's best.[[152]](#cite_note-152)[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:San_Antonio_Spurs#Dubious "Talk:San Antonio Spurs")*] The two teams met in the playoffs five times in six seasons, and one of the two teams advanced to the NBA Finals every year from 1999 to 2005.[[153]](#cite_note-153)

### Dallas Mavericks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio_Spurs&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Dallas Mavericks")]

The rivalry between the San Antonio Spurs and the [Dallas Mavericks](/wiki/Dallas_Mavericks "Dallas Mavericks") features two teams with Dallas roots. The Mavericks were swept in the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_NBA_season "2012–13 NBA season") by the Spurs for the first time since the [1998 season](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_NBA_season "1997–98 NBA season"), Tim Duncan's rookie season. In their last matchup of the season, San Antonio escaped with a 95–94 victory over Dallas when a [Vince Carter](/wiki/Vince_Carter "Vince Carter") attempt bounced off the rim at the buzzer. With that win, the Spurs clinched a playoff spot for a 16th straight season. San Antonio also reached 50 wins for a 14th consecutive season, the longest streak in NBA history.[[154]](#cite_note-154)

### Phoenix Suns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio_Spurs&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Phoenix Suns")]

Since 1992, the Spurs and the [Phoenix Suns](/wiki/Phoenix_Suns "Phoenix Suns") have met 10 times in the playoffs, in which the Spurs have won six series and the Suns have won four. Some of the most notable moments of their rivalry include [Charles Barkley](/wiki/Charles_Barkley "Charles Barkley")'s game-winning jump shot in the [1993 playoffs](/wiki/1993_NBA_playoffs "1993 NBA playoffs") in the final game at the [HemisFair Arena](/wiki/HemisFair_Arena "HemisFair Arena"),[[155]](#cite_note-155) [Stephon Marbury](/wiki/Stephon_Marbury "Stephon Marbury")'s buzzer-beating three-pointer in Game 1 of the first round of the [2003 playoffs](/wiki/2003_NBA_playoffs "2003 NBA playoffs"),[[156]](#cite_note-156) [Robert Horry](/wiki/Robert_Horry "Robert Horry")'s hip check on [Steve Nash](/wiki/Steve_Nash "Steve Nash") in Game 4 of the [2007 playoffs](/wiki/2007_NBA_playoffs "2007 NBA playoffs"),[[157]](#cite_note-157) Tim Duncan's game-tying three-pointer in Game 1 of the [2008 first round](/wiki/2008_NBA_playoffs "2008 NBA playoffs"),[[158]](#cite_note-158) and [Goran Dragić](/wiki/Goran_Dragi%C4%87 "Goran Dragić")'s 23-point outburst in the fourth quarter of Game 3 in the West semifinals of the [2010 playoffs](/wiki/2010_NBA_playoffs "2010 NBA playoffs").[[159]](#cite_note-159)

### Houston Rockets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio_Spurs&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Houston Rockets")]

The rivalry between the Spurs and the [Houston Rockets](/wiki/Houston_Rockets "Houston Rockets") was renewed in the [2017 playoffs](/wiki/2017_NBA_playoffs "2017 NBA playoffs"), in which the two teams met in the Western Conference Semifinals. The match-up was the first between the two teams in the playoffs since the 1995 Western Conference Finals.[[160]](#cite_note-160) In Game Two of the series, starting point guard [Tony Parker](/wiki/Tony_Parker "Tony Parker") suffered a ruptured quadriceps tendon, forcing him to miss the remainder of the playoffs.[[161]](#cite_note-161) In Game Five, all-star small forward [Kawhi Leonard](/wiki/Kawhi_Leonard "Kawhi Leonard") suffered an injury to his right ankle in the third quarter, resulting in him sitting out for the closing portions of the game. Despite the injury issues, the Spurs were able to send Game Five to overtime. In the overtime period, [Manu Ginóbili](/wiki/Manu_Gin%C3%B3bili "Manu Ginóbili") blocked [James Harden](/wiki/James_Harden "James Harden")'s three-point attempt in the final seconds to secure the 110–107 victory for the Spurs.[[162]](#cite_note-162) The Spurs would close out the series in a Game Six with a 114–75 win.[[163]](#cite_note-163)

### Oklahoma City Thunder

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio_Spurs&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Oklahoma City Thunder")]

The rivalry between the San Antonio Spurs and [Oklahoma City Thunder](/wiki/Oklahoma_City_Thunder "Oklahoma City Thunder"), which defined the Western Conference in the 2010s, has been reborn in the mid-2020s, driven by the emergence of [Victor Wembanyama](/wiki/Victor_Wembanyama "Victor Wembanyama") and the rise of a young Thunder squad. While the early 2010s focused on a clash of styles (Spurs' experience vs. Thunder's athleticism), the current era is defined by young, top-tier talent facing off in a division matchup that has recently seen the Spurs dominate the defending-champion Thunder.[[164]](#cite_note-164)

## Logo and uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio_Spurs&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Logo and uniforms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/San_Antonio_Spurs_wordmark_logo.gif/250px-San_Antonio_Spurs_wordmark_logo.gif)](/wiki/File:San_Antonio_Spurs_wordmark_logo.gif)

The San Antonio Spurs' previous wordmark logo

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Willie_Anderson_jersey.jpg/250px-Willie_Anderson_jersey.jpg)](/wiki/File:Willie_Anderson_jersey.jpg)

The Spurs sported radially arched black-and-white letters on their uniforms during much of the 1980s.[[165]](#cite_note-GoingRetro-165)

Since becoming the San Antonio Spurs in 1973, the team colors have been black, silver and white. The distinctive logo of the word Spurs in [Eurostile](/wiki/Eurostile "Eurostile") font, with the stylized [spur](/wiki/Spur "Spur") substituting for the letter U, has been a part of the team's identity since their move to San Antonio.[[165]](#cite_note-GoingRetro-165)[[166]](#cite_note-166) The logo incorporated 'Fiesta colors' of pink, orange and turquoise, used from 1989 to 2002 (though the uniforms remained the same), and alignment from straight to arched beginning with the [2002–03 NBA season](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_NBA_season "2002–03 NBA season").

The Spurs have always worn black on the road and white at home, except during the 1973–76 [ABA](/wiki/American_Basketball_Association "American Basketball Association") seasons and their first NBA season when the home uniform was always silver. Until the [1988–89 NBA season](/wiki/1988%E2%80%9389_NBA_season "1988–89 NBA season"), the road uniform had "San Antonio" on the front while the home uniform featured the team nickname adopted from the Spurs logo; from 1973 to 1982, the road uniform lettering was black with silver trim. In addition, from 1977 to 1981 a [saddle](/wiki/Saddle "Saddle")-like striping was featured on the back of the home shorts. Since the [1989–90 NBA season](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_NBA_season "1989–90 NBA season") the Spurs uniform has remained practically the same, with the road uniform now using the team nickname from their logo; a minor change included the addition of another black (road) and white (home) trim to the already silver-trimmed block numbers in the 2002–03 season. In 2003–04 and later in 2006–07, they wore silver throwback jerseys to honor their late 1970s team. The Spurs wear black sneakers and socks on the road, and white sneakers and socks at home (except for select games with the silver alternates), a practice that began in the 2002–03 season. When the NBA moved to the [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") *Revolution 30* technology for the 2010–11 season, the Spurs changed to V-neck jerseys and eliminated striping on the shorts' beltline.

On September 19, 2012, the Spurs unveiled a silver alternate uniform. In breaking from the traditional practice of placing the team or city name in front, the Spurs' new uniform features only the stylized spur logo, with the black number trimmed in white and silver on the upper right. The Spurs primary logo is atop the player name and number on the back (replaced by the NBA logo prior to the 2014–15 season), while the Eurostile 'SA' initials (for San Antonio) are on the left leg of the shorts. They also wore ABA throwbacks of Chaparrals team. Black, silver and white side stripes are also featured on the uniform. The uniforms are worn for select home games.[[167]](#cite_note-167) A variation of this uniform, featuring [military camouflage](/wiki/Military_camouflage "Military camouflage") patterns instead of the usual silver, was used for two games in the 2013–14 season; a sleeved version was used the next season.[[168]](#cite_note-168) Another variation, this time in black, was unveiled for the 2015–16 season.[[169]](#cite_note-169)

At times throughout the season, the Spurs wear a jersey that says "Los Spurs" on the front, in recognition of Latino fans both at home and across the US and Latin America. The Spurs (located in a city with a large Hispanic population) were one of the first NBA teams to wear these branded jerseys. In 2014, the jerseys were sleeved. These events are called "Noches Latinas", first launched during the 2006–07 NBA season, part of a Hispanic marketing campaign known as "éne-bé-a".[[170]](#cite_note-170) Six teams in the NBA participate in these events. The Spurs have had the most players from Latin America and are one of only three NBA teams who have had at least five players on their rosters who originate from Latin America and Spain (if one includes Puerto Rico as part of Latin America, although it is a U.S. territory), the others being the Memphis Grizzlies and the Portland Trail Blazers.[[171]](#cite_note-171)

The switch to [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc.") as the uniform provider in 2017 eliminated the "home" and "away" uniform designations. The Spurs' black "Icon", silver "Statement" and white "Association" uniform remained identical to the previous set save for the manufacturer's logo and switch from Eurostile to custom block lettering on the team name. They also continued to wear their camouflage-style uniforms as part of the "City" edition.[[172]](#cite_note-172) However, the Spurs retired their camouflage-style "City" uniforms prior to the 2020–21 season in favor of a design influenced by the team's 1989–2002 "Fiesta" era.[[173]](#cite_note-173) This design was brought back in the 2025–26 season after the NBA chose to revisit past "City" uniform designs for that season.[[174]](#cite_note-174) The Spurs reprised the "Fiesta" theme for the 2021–22 season, but mixed in a few elements from previous uniforms. The shorts featured turquoise diamonds in homage to the George Gervin-era uniforms, and the logo on the left leg honored the Dallas Chaparrals.[[175]](#cite_note-175) In 2022, the Spurs replaced their silver "Statement" uniforms with a black one, but with silver letters, a new "SATX" wordmark, a new "Texas spur" alternate logo, and grayscale patterns inspired from traditional [serapes](/wiki/Serape "Serape") and [saddle blankets](/wiki/Saddle_blanket "Saddle blanket").[[176]](#cite_note-176) Also during the season, the Spurs wore a turquoise "City" uniform with elements based on the uniforms worn in the [1996 NBA All-Star Game](/wiki/1996_NBA_All-Star_Game "1996 NBA All-Star Game").[[177]](#cite_note-177) The "City" uniform in the 2023–24 season paid homage to [Hemisfair '68](/wiki/Hemisfair_%2768 "Hemisfair '68"), featuring a white base, tan and orange stripes. Also featured is a psychedelic-style "San Antonio" wordmark in brown along with neon light-inspired numbers in sand, white and brown accents.[[178]](#cite_note-178) The Hemisfair-themed design, albeit in light blue, was reused for the 2024–25 "City" uniform.[[179]](#cite_note-179)

In the 2025–26 season, the Spurs released a new "Statement" uniform, returning to a silver base but with a block "San Antonio" on the chest. This marked the first time since the 1988–89 season that the city name was featured on a core Spurs uniform.[[180]](#cite_note-180)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Spurs_Coyote_sign.JPG/250px-Spurs_Coyote_sign.JPG)](/wiki/File:Spurs_Coyote_sign.JPG)

The Coyote showing a sign to the crowd during a [time-out](/wiki/Time-out_(sport) "Time-out (sport)") at a Spurs game

### Mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio_Spurs&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Mascot")]

[The Coyote](/wiki/The_Coyote_(mascot) "The Coyote (mascot)") has been the Spurs' mascot since 1983. Created by and portrayed for 21 years by Tim Derk,[[181]](#cite_note-181) Coyote is known for his slapstick humor, rehearsed skits and previous dance numbers with Spurs' cheerleaders, the *Silver Dancers*, who were replaced in 2018 after 26 years.[[182]](#cite_note-182) The Coyote was a 2007 inductee into the [Mascot Hall of Fame](/wiki/Mascot_Hall_of_Fame "Mascot Hall of Fame") and has been featured in local and national media.[[183]](#cite_note-MHoF-183)

### Retained draft rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Antonio_Spurs&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Retained draft rights")]

The Spurs own the NBA rights to the players listed in the table below. The typical pattern is to allow the player to develop in leagues outside the United States. The player is free to negotiate contracts in other leagues and is not obligated to play in the NBA. Sometimes, a player's overseas contract may have an expensive buyout clause that would discourage the Spurs from seeking to bring him in. The Spurs have had past success in finding foreign talent; some examples of this success include the selections of second-rounder [Manu Ginóbili](/wiki/Manu_Gin%C3%B3bili "Manu Ginóbili") (57th overall in [1999](/wiki/1999_NBA_draft "1999 NBA draft")) and first-rounder [Tony Parker](/wiki/Tony_Parker "Tony Parker") (28th overall in [2001](/wiki/2001_NBA_draft "2001 NBA draft")), who both went on to become [All-Stars](/wiki/NBA_All-Star_Weekend "NBA All-Star Weekend").
